questions.question: At 64K-token context, what fraction of total decode latency does the NSA paper attribute to attention, and why does this make sparse attention an infrastructure-level priority rather than a research curiosity?
options: About 10–15% — notable but not the dominant bottleneck; compute-bound FFN layers still dominate
About 30–40% — significant enough to warrant optimisation after other bottlenecks are addressed
About 50% — at the halfway point where sparse attention breaks even with optimised dense attention
About 70–80% — attention dominates, so every downstream metric (TTFT, tokens/sec, cost per million tokens) is bounded by how fast you can serve attention at long context
questions.explanation: The NSA paper (Yuan et al., ACL 2025 Best Paper) measured 70–80% of decode latency in attention at 64K context. When one operation consumes 4/5 of all compute, no amount of FFN or embedding optimisation can move the needle — you must fix attention. This is why NSA's 9× speedup over FlashAttention at 64K translates directly to ~7× overall decode speedup.

questions.question: NSA runs three parallel attention branches for each query. Match each branch to its purpose and the type of context it captures.
options: Compressed branch = coarse global context via block-averaged summary tokens; Selected branch = fine-grained attention on the top-k most relevant blocks; Sliding-window branch = local context for the most recent W tokens
Compressed branch = recent local tokens in sliding window; Selected branch = global coarse summary; Sliding-window branch = top-k relevant sparse blocks
All three branches capture identical context at different compression ratios; the gate learns which ratio is best per query
Compressed branch = top-k relevant blocks; Selected branch = recent local tokens; Sliding-window branch = full-sequence coarse summary
questions.explanation: NSA's three branches cover different receptive fields: (1) Compressed — the entire sequence but averaged into N/l block summaries, giving cheap global awareness; (2) Selected — the full uncompressed tokens from the k blocks with the highest compressed-branch scores, giving precise fine-grained retrieval; (3) Sliding window — the last W tokens for local coherence. The gate weights their contributions per position.

questions.question: Prior sparse-attention methods (H2O, StreamingLLM) pruned the KV cache at inference time but underperformed NSA. What makes NSA 'natively trainable' where inference-time pruning is not?
options: NSA uses a fixed block-sparse pattern expressible as a standard causal mask, making gradient computation straightforward
The top-k block selection is replaced with a differentiable soft-selection during training and hardened to discrete top-k at inference

Gradients flow through the differentiable compressed-branch attention, which also determines the top-k selection — the model learns to route information through the sparse pattern from the start of pre-training, not as an overlay on dense-attention weights
NSA uses special scale-and-shift position embeddings that allow the model to learn which tokens to route without any discrete selection operation
questions.explanation: H2O and StreamingLLM prune the KV cache of a model trained expecting to attend to all tokens — the weights have no incentive to route information to accommodate the pruning pattern. NSA's compressed branch is a differentiable attention over the entire sequence; its scores drive the top-k selection. Gradients flow through compressed-branch scores end-to-end, so the model learns during pre-training to put the information NSA needs into the selected blocks.

questions.question: At N=64K context with l=64 (compression blocks), k=16 (top-k), b=64 (selected block size), w=512 (sliding window), NSA sees 1000+1024+512=2536 keys per query vs 64000 for full attention. The doc says the savings ratio grows with sequence length. Why?
options: At longer sequences fewer blocks qualify for top-k selection, automatically reducing the selected-branch key count

The compressed-branch cost is O(N/l); the selected and sliding branches are O(k·b) and O(w), both constants — as N doubles the compressed branch doubles but the total grows more slowly than O(N), widening the ratio
The sliding window W is automatically expanded at longer contexts, but its cost grows sub-linearly because tokens repeat
At longer sequences the gate MLP learns to down-weight the selected branch, reducing the k·b key loads proportionally
questions.explanation: Full attention cost is O(N). NSA cost = O(N/l) + O(k·b) + O(w). At 64K: 1000 + 1024 + 512 = 2536 (25× reduction). At 128K: 2000 + 1024 + 512 = 3536 (36× reduction). The selected and sliding terms are constant in N; only the compressed branch grows — but it grows as N/l not N. So the ratio NSA/full shrinks as N grows, and the savings ratio (full/NSA) grows. The paper shows this benefit scaling linearly with sequence length.

questions.question: The Build code Step 6 on a 1024-token synthetic with l=32, k=4, w=128 prints NSA sees 288 keys vs 1024 for full attention. Account for where the 288 comes from.
options: Compressed branch: 1024/32 = 32 block summaries; Selected branch: k×b = 4×32 = 128 fine-grained tokens; Sliding window: w = 128 recent tokens. Total: 32+128+128 = 288
Compressed branch: k×b = 4×32 = 128; Selected branch: 1024/32 = 32; Sliding window: 128. Total: 128+32+128 = 288
Compressed branch: w = 128; Selected branch: 1024/32 = 32; Sliding window: k×b = 128. Total: 128+32+128 = 288
All 288 come from the compressed branch at three different compression ratios: 32+128+128 = 288
questions.explanation: N/l = 1024/32 = 32 compressed block summaries for the compressed branch. k×b = 4 selected blocks × 32 tokens each = 128 fine-grained tokens for the selected branch. w = 128 most recent tokens for the sliding window. 32+128+128 = 288 total keys per query, versus 1024 for full attention — a 3.5× reduction even at short context. At 64K this ratio grows to 25× and at 128K to 36×.

questions.question: The doc distinguishes NSA from MoBA (Moonshot, arXiv:2502.13189) as 'the two architectures to know for 2026 long-context pre-training.' Both use three branches or block-level routing. What is the conceptual lineage each draws from, and when does the doc say you should NOT reach for NSA?
options: NSA draws from sparse attention research (Longformer, BigBird); MoBA draws from Mixture-of-Experts. Avoid NSA when context is always under 16K or when serving existing dense-attention weights that were not pre-trained with NSA
NSA draws from diffusion models; MoBA draws from FlashAttention. Avoid NSA for batch-1 CPU deployments
NSA draws from KV cache pruning; MoBA draws from residual VQ tokenisation. Avoid NSA when using speculative decoding
NSA and MoBA share identical conceptual lineage from routing transformers; avoid NSA when using GQA because the two are incompatible
questions.explanation: NSA is the sparse-attention line (Longformer → BigBird → NSA with learned sparsity); MoBA applies the MoE principle (mixture of experts) to attention blocks. The doc's 'When not to' section: NSA requires continued training to retrofit on an existing model (cannot bolt on at inference like KV pruning), doesn't help below 16K (three-branch overhead dominates savings), and is not justified for batch-1 interactive short-context chat.
